title: माण्डूक्योपनिषद्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री माण्डूक्योपनिषद् ॥

॥ शान्ति मन्त्र ॥
ॐ भद्रं कर्णेभिः शृणुयाम देवाः भद्रं पश्येमाक्षभिर्यजत्राः।
स्थिरैरङ्गैस्तुष्टुवांसस्तनूभिर्व्यशेम देवहितं यदायुः॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥

॥ उपनिषद् ॥
ॐ इत्येतदक्षरमिदं सर्वं तस्योपव्याख्यानं
भूतं भवद्भविष्यदिति सर्वमोंकार एव।
यच्चान्यत्त्रिकालातीतं तदप्योंकार एव॥ १॥

सर्वं ह्येतद्ब्रह्मायमात्मा ब्रह्म सोऽयमात्मा चतुष्पात्॥ २॥

जागरितस्थानो बहिष्प्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः
स्थूलभुग्वैश्वानरः प्रथमः पादः॥ ३॥

स्वप्नस्थानोऽन्तःप्रज्ञः सप्ताङ्ग एकोनविंशतिमुखः
प्रविविक्तभुक्तैजसो द्वितीयः पादः॥ ४॥

यत्र सुप्तो न कञ्चन कामं कामयते न कञ्चन स्वप्नं पश्यति
तत्सुषुप्तम्। सुषुप्तस्थान एकीभूतः प्रज्ञानघन
एवानन्दमयो ह्यानन्दभुक्चेतोमुखः प्राज्ञस्तृतीयः पादः॥ ५॥

एष सर्वेश्वरः एष सर्वज्ञ एषोऽन्तर्यामी
एष योनिः सर्वस्य प्रभवाप्ययौ हि भूतानाम्॥ ६॥

नान्तःप्रज्ञं न बहिष्प्रज्ञं नोभयतःप्रज्ञं
न प्रज्ञानघनं न प्रज्ञं नाप्रज्ञम्।
अदृष्टमव्यवहार्यमग्राह्यमलक्षणम्
अचिन्त्यमव्यपदेश्यमेकात्मप्रत्ययसारं
प्रपञ्चोपशमं शान्तं शिवमद्वैतं
चतुर्थं मन्यन्ते स आत्मा स विज्ञेयः॥ ७॥

सोऽयमात्माध्यक्षरमोंकारोऽधिमात्रं पादा मात्रा
मात्राश्च पादा अकार उकारो मकार इति॥ ८॥

जागरितस्थानो वैश्वानरोऽकारः प्रथमा मात्रा
आप्तेरादिमत्त्वाद्वाऽऽप्नोति ह वै सर्वान्कामानादिश्च
भवति य एवं वेद॥ ९॥

स्वप्नस्थानस्तैजस उकारो द्वितीया मात्रा
उत्कर्षादुभयत्वाद्वोत्कर्षति ह वै ज्ञानसन्ततिं
समानश्च भवति नास्याब्रह्मवित्कुले भवति य एवं वेद॥ १०॥

सुषुप्तस्थानः प्राज्ञो मकारस्तृतीया मात्रा
मितेरपीतेर्वा मिनोति ह वै सर्वमिदं
अपीतिश्च भवति य एवं वेद॥ ११॥

अमात्रश्चतुर्थोऽव्यवहार्यः प्रपञ्चोपशमः
शिवोऽद्वैत एवमोंकार आत्मैव
संविशत्यात्मनाऽऽत्मानं य एवं वेद य एवं वेद॥ १२॥

॥ गौडपाद कारिका — प्रमुख श्लोक ॥

विश्वो हि स्थूलभुन्नित्यं तैजसः प्रविविक्तभुक्।
आनन्दभुक्तथा प्राज्ञस्त्रिधा भोगं निबोधत॥

स्थूलं तर्पयते विश्वं प्रविविक्तं तु तैजसम्।
आनन्दश्च तथा प्राज्ञं त्रिधा तृप्तिं निबोधत॥

न निरोधो न चोत्पत्तिर्न बद्धो न च साधकः।
न मुमुक्षुर्न वै मुक्त इत्येषा परमार्थता॥

॥ शान्ति मन्त्र ॥
ॐ भद्रं कर्णेभिः शृणुयाम देवाः भद्रं पश्येमाक्षभिर्यजत्राः।
स्थिरैरङ्गैस्तुष्टुवांसस्तनूभिर्व्यशेम देवहितं यदायुः॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥

॥ इति श्री माण्डूक्योपनिषद् सम्पूर्णा ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Mandukyopanishad ||

|| Shanti Mantra ||
Om Bhadram Karnebhih Shrinuyama Devah Bhadram Pashyema-akshabhir-yajatrah.
Sthirair-angais-tushtuvamsas-tanubhir-vyashema Deva-hitam Yad-ayuh.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.

|| Upanishad ||
Om Ity-etad-aksharam-idam Sarvam Tasya-upavyakhyanam
Bhutam Bhavad-bhavishyad-iti Sarvam-Onkara Eva.
Yach-cha-anyat-trikalatitam Tad-apy-Onkara Eva. || 1 ||

Sarvam Hy-etad-Brahma-ayam-atma Brahma So-ayam-atma Chatushpat. || 2 ||

Jagarita-sthano Bahish-prajnah Saptanga Ekonavimshatim-ukhah
Sthula-bhug-Vaishvanarah Prathamah Padah. || 3 ||

Svapna-sthano-antah-prajnah Saptanga Ekonavimshatim-ukhah
Pravi-vikta-bhuk-Taijaso Dvitiyah Padah. || 4 ||

Yatra Supto Na Kanchana Kamam Kamayate Na Kanchana Svapnam Pashyati
Tat-sushuptam. Sushuptas-thana Ekibhutah Prajnana-ghana
Eva-anandamayo Hy-ananda-bhuk-chetomukah Prajnas-tritiyah Padah. || 5 ||

Esha Sarveshvarah Esha Sarvajna Esho-antaryami
Esha Yonih Sarvasya Prabhava-apyayau Hi Bhutanam. || 6 ||

Na-antah-prajnam Na Bahish-prajnam No-ubhayatah-prajnam
Na Prajnana-ghanam Na Prajnam Na-aprajnam.
Adrishtam-avyavaharyam-agrahyam-alakshanam
Achintyam-avyapadeshyam-ekatma-pratyaya-saram
Prapanchopasamam Shantam Shivam-advaitam
Chaturtham Manyante Sa Atma Sa Vijneyah. || 7 ||

So-ayam-atma-adhyaksharam-Onkaro-adhimatram Pada Matra
Matrash-cha Pada Akara Ukaro Makara Iti. || 8 ||

Jagarita-sthaano Vaishvanaro-akarah Prathama Matra
Aapter-aadimattvad-va-aapnoti Ha Vai Sarvan-kaman-adish-cha
Bhavati Ya Evam Veda. || 9 ||

Svapna-sthanas-taijasa Ukaro Dvitiya Matra
Utkarshad-ubhayatvad-votkashati Ha Vai Jnana-santatim
Samanash-cha Bhavati Na-asya-abrahmavit-kule Bhavati Ya Evam Veda. || 10 ||

Sushuptas-thanah Prajno Makaras-tritiya Matra
Miter-apiter-va Minoti Ha Vai Sarvam-idam
Apitish-cha Bhavati Ya Evam Veda. || 11 ||

Amatrash-chaturtho-avyavaharyah Prapanchopasamah
Shivo-advaita Evam-Onkara Atmaiva
Samvishaty-atmana-atmanam Ya Evam Veda Ya Evam Veda. || 12 ||

|| Gaudapada Karika — Pramukh Shloka ||

Vishvo Hi Sthula-bhun-nityam Taijasah Pravi-vikta-bhuk.
Ananda-bhuk-tatha Prajnas-tridha Bhogam Nibodhata.

Sthulam Tarpayate Vishvam Praviviktam Tu Taijasam.
Anandash-cha Tatha Prajnam Tridha Triptim Nibodhata.

Na Nirodho Na Chotpattir-na Baddho Na Cha Sadhakah.
Na Mumukshur-na Vai Mukta Ity-esha Paramarthata.

|| Shanti Mantra ||
Om Bhadram Karnebhih Shrinuyama Devah Bhadram Pashyema-akshabhir-yajatrah.
Sthirair-angais-tushtuvamsas-tanubhir-vyashema Deva-hitam Yad-ayuh.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.

|| Iti Shri Mandukyopanishad Sampurna ||
hindiMeaning:
माण्डूक्योपनिषद् अथर्ववेद से सम्बन्धित सबसे छोटी (12 मन्त्र) किन्तु सर्वाधिक गहन उपनिषद् है। गौडपादाचार्य (शंकराचार्य के परम गुरु) ने इस पर कारिका लिखी।

यह उपनिषद् ओंकार (ॐ) की व्याख्या के माध्यम से आत्मा के चार पादों (अवस्थाओं) का वर्णन करती है:

1. वैश्वानर (जाग्रत अवस्था) — अकार — बाह्य प्रज्ञ, स्थूल भोक्ता
2. तैजस (स्वप्न अवस्था) — उकार — अन्तः प्रज्ञ, सूक्ष्म भोक्ता
3. प्राज्ञ (सुषुप्ति अवस्था) — मकार — प्रज्ञानघन, आनन्दभोक्ता
4. तुरीय (चतुर्थ अवस्था) — अमात्र ओंकार — न अन्तः प्रज्ञ, न बहिः प्रज्ञ, अदृष्ट, अव्यवहार्य, अग्राह्य, अचिन्त्य, प्रपञ्चोपशम, शान्त, शिव, अद्वैत — यही आत्मा है, यही जानने योग्य है।

गौडपाद कारिका का प्रसिद्ध श्लोक कहता है — न निरोध है, न उत्पत्ति, न बद्ध है, न साधक, न मुमुक्षु है, न मुक्त — यही परमार्थ सत्य है (अजातिवाद)।
benefits: Most concise yet profound Upanishad - called sufficient for liberation by itself
Reveals the four states of consciousness through analysis of Om
Turiya (the Fourth) transcends waking, dream, and deep sleep states
Gaudapada Karika key verses establish the Ajativada (non-origination) philosophy
Foundation of Advaita Vedanta as taught by Shankaracharya
Meditation on Mandukya leads to direct realization of Atman as Brahman
description: Dedicated to Lord Vishnu, the Mandukya Upanishad (माण्डूक्योपनिषद्) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Atharvaveda - Mandukya Upanishad, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to meditation on Mandukya leads to direct realization of Atman as Brahman, reveal the four states of consciousness through analysis of Om, and turiya (the Fourth) transcends waking, dream, and deep sleep states. With 12 verses and a reading time of about 15 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Mandukya Upanishad in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Mandukya Upanishad in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Complete 12 mantras with Gaudapada Karika key verses on Om and four states of consciousness.

Four states consciousness Vaishvanara Taijasa Prajna Turiya
Turiya the Fourth state Mandukya verse 7 silence
AUM analysis Akara Ukara Makara Amatra Mandukya
Bhadram Karnebhih Atharvaveda Shanti Mantra Mandukya

seoFaqs.question: What is the Mandukya Upanishad?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mandukya Upanishad (माण्डूक्योपनिषद्) is the shortest of the ten principal Upanishads, comprising just 12 mantras yet considered by Advaita Vedanta to be the most profound. It belongs to the Atharva Veda and is named after its rishi-seer Manduka (Manduka-rishi means frog-sage; possibly an allusion to its leap-like compactness from one consciousness-state to another). The Upanishad is unique in being entirely focused on a single subject — the cosmic syllable Om / Aum — analyzing it as the symbol of the four states (chatushpat) of Atman: Vaishvanara (waking), Taijasa (dream), Prajna (deep sleep) and Turiya (the Fourth — pure Self). Its sister-text, the Gaudapada Karika (215 verses by Sage Gaudapada — Adi Shankaracharya's parama-guru), is the foundational Karika of Advaita Vedanta. Together they constitute the most concise expression of non-dual Brahman-Atman identity. (Source: Atharva Veda — Mandukya Upanishad.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the four states of consciousness in Mandukya?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mandukya Upanishad enumerates the chatushpat (four-quartered) Atman as four progressively-deeper states of consciousness: (1) Vaishvanara (वैश्वानर) — the waking-state Atman, akara (अ-कार, the A of Aum), bahish-prajnah (outward-aware), sthula-bhuk (gross-enjoyer of physical objects), seven-limbed and nineteen-mouthed (verse 3); (2) Taijasa (तैजस) — the dream-state Atman, ukara (उ-कार, the U of Aum), antah-prajnah (inward-aware), pravivikta-bhuk (subtle-enjoyer of dream objects) (verse 4); (3) Prajna (प्राज्ञ) — the deep-sleep Atman, makara (म-कार, the M of Aum), prajnana-ghana (consciousness-mass), ananda-bhuk (bliss-enjoyer) (verse 5); (4) Turiya (तुरीय — The Fourth) — amatra (without-letter), neither inward-aware nor outward-aware, neither consciousness-mass nor unconscious, adrishta (unseen), avyavaharya (untransacted), agrahya (ungraspable), achintya (unthinkable), Shantam Shivam Advaitam (peaceful, auspicious, non-dual) — this is the Atman that is to be realized (verse 7). (Source: Mandukya Upanishad verses 2-7.)

seoFaqs.question: Why does the Mandukya Upanishad analyze the syllable Om?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mandukya Upanishad opens (verse 1) with Om ity-etad-aksharam-idam sarvam — Om is this aksharam (immutable syllable, also meaning letter), all this — past, present and future — is verily Om. The entire Upanishad then maps Om's three letters (Akara A, Ukara U, Makara M) plus its silent fourth (Amatra — the silence that follows after Om is chanted) onto the four states of Atman: A=Vaishvanara/waking, U=Taijasa/dream, M=Prajna/deep-sleep, Amatra-silence=Turiya. By meditating on Om as encompassing all four states, the seeker progressively recognizes that all four states arise in and dissolve into the same single Atman, which is identical with Brahman. The Mandukya thus uses Om as the meditation-vehicle (pranava-upasana) for direct realization of Atman-Brahman identity — making it a complete moksha-shastra in 12 verses. (Sources: Mandukya Upanishad verses 1, 8-12; Pranava upasana tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Gaudapada Karika and why is it important?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mandukya Karika (माण्डूक्य कारिका, also called Agama Shastra) is a 215-verse commentary on the Mandukya Upanishad composed by Sage Gaudapadacharya — the parama-guru (teacher's teacher) of Adi Shankaracharya through the lineage Gaudapada → Govinda Bhagavatpada → Shankara. The Karika is divided into four prakaranas (sections): (1) Agama-prakarana — exposition of the Mandukya text; (2) Vaitathya-prakarana — establishment of the unreality of the dual world; (3) Advaita-prakarana — establishment of non-duality; (4) Alata-shanti-prakarana — the famous fire-brand (alata) analogy showing the world appears like the circle made by a whirling fire-brand — illusory motion in stationary fire. Gaudapada's central doctrine is Ajativada (अजातिवाद — the doctrine of non-origination): nothing is ever truly born, hence nothing dies, all duality is appearance only. The famous verse Na nirodho na chotpattir-na baddho na cha sadhakah, na mumukshur-na vai mukta — neither bondage nor liberation, neither seeker nor freed — is paramartha-satya. (Sources: Mandukya Karika; Gaudapada-Shankara lineage.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Mandukya called sufficient for liberation by itself?
seoFaqs.answer: The Muktika Upanishad (a late Upanishad enumerating all 108 Upanishads) declares: Mandukyam-ekam-eva-alam mumukshunam vimuktaye — Mandukya alone is sufficient (alam) for the liberation (vimuktaye) of seekers (mumukshunam). The reasoning is: (1) The Mandukya is the most concise complete Brahmavidya — 12 verses cover the entire Vedanta in compressed form; (2) It maps every state of consciousness (waking, dream, deep-sleep, Turiya) leaving nothing of the human experience outside its analysis; (3) Through Om-meditation it provides a direct upasana methodology, not merely theory; (4) The Gaudapada Karika expansion provides complete Advaita Vedanta; (5) Adi Shankaracharya wrote a bhashya specifically commenting on the Karika, calling it the foundation of Advaita; (6) The phala-shruti (verse 12) — Samvishaty-atmana-atmanam ya evam veda — whoever knows this enters the Atman by the Atman — promises direct moksha to the realizer. Hence the Mandukya is treated as the seed-Upanishad from which the others are commentaries. (Sources: Muktika Upanishad; Advaita Vedanta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Mandukya Upanishad be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mandukya Upanishad has no specific tithi-bound mandate (universal recitation), but is most powerful: at sandhya (especially in conjunction with Aum-japa), as a daily Upanishad-svadhyaya, on Vyasa Purnima / Guru Purnima (the day of guru-veneration, especially for honoring Gaudapada-Shankara lineage), Adi Shankara Jayanti (Vaishakh Shukla Panchami — the birth anniversary of Shankaracharya), at the start of any Advaita Vedanta study, before any Pranava-meditation (since Mandukya is the Pranava-Upanishad), as preparation for nirvikalpa samadhi practice, at any moment of self-inquiry (atma-vichara) when investigating I-am-awareness, before any major Vedanta-vichara, and as part of Dasha-Upanishad parayana. Many Advaita teachers (Ramana Maharshi, Nisargadatta Maharaj, Swami Dayananda Saraswati) regard Mandukya as the highest Upanishadic teaching. The full recitation takes about 15 minutes. (Sources: Advaita Vedanta tradition; Upanishad parayana paddhati.)
